I had come to this seminar. I was so excited that I got to just relax, be like everybody else. I had no responsibility. I didn't have to do anything until Dan saw me this morning. And he said, I have something for you to do. But he did not put it here. So what we're going to do is we're going to get ready and be entertained by five videos. So in your workbook, if you would go to six videos, thank you very much. I know it's five here. Great. Go to page 58. Okay. On 58, you can start to take some notes. So the first video is going to be two newsreels of Houdini doing his famous straight jacket escape. The second video is going to be how to demonstrate any intangible, and this is Dan's demo from the Daily Cash in Your Mailbox infomercial. The third and fourth video are going to be from Goal by the Inch infomercial, which holds the record for the longest consecutive airing of Pure Lead Generation Show. Every day, somewhere in the United States for eight solid years. And both of these are going to be tracking the challenges of demonstrating an intangible. Now I am not seeing this, but I will, I might be embarrassed here because I used to do some of those infomercials. And just so you know, if I did them, I was like 18 or so. So give me a little break. If you believe that, I have some really good land for you. The next video is going to be from the reality show Pitchman, which is a very dramatic demonstration. So we are going to enjoy ourselves with videos for a little while. So roll it. Would you please bring in the mail? Set it right down here. Want to bring the table over? Now folks, what I have done is for the last couple of days, just save the mail from my office and had it shipped here so that I could have it opened up right before you to see what it is like to receive daily cash in the mail. And look at this. This is mail from all over the country. We are not going to open it all up because it will be all over everywhere. Now listen, I need some volunteers. First of all, is there anyone that knows how to operate a calculator very quickly? Okay sir, would you please come down? And then I need, thank you. And I need some other people that will come and help me feel this excitement of opening daily cash. You, you, okay and you sir, come on down. Come on down here. Okay, now what I want you to do is as quickly as you can is open up the envelope and just reach in and take the check out, keep it with the envelope and then what's your name sir? Andre. Pass it over to Andre so that he can start adding it up, okay? Okay now listen to this folks. Okay here's a check for $39.95. How much is your check for? Okay we got $39.95. Okay, another one for $39. $40. $40. Okay, another one for $39. Okay, listen to this folks. $39.40, $50, $106, $5, $39, $50, $5, $10, $12, $39, $40, daily cash in your mailbox. How much? $49.25. $89.25. Andres, how much money do we have? Damn, so far we've got $600. $600? And just a few minutes and open them in daily cash. This is a demonstration of how to sell your jewelry. It is based on the most common questions you will be asked and intended to be only a basic sales presentation. Can you tell me what this is? It's 14 karat gold layer. What does that mean? It means that 14 karat gold has been layered over a semi-precious jeweler's metal. Will it wear off? No, most people wear it for years without any visible change. However, it does come with a manufacturer's lifetime guarantee. If at any time you have any problems with a chain, including breaking a clasp, just send it back directly to U.S. gold chain and they will replace it at no charge except for postage and handling. Can you make a necklace at any length I want? I can make one at the exact length while you wait. I can do the same for bracelets and anklets. When you buy a chain by the inch, you will always have a custom fit. How much is it? It's sold by the inch, so you only pay for what you need. I'll measure the chain and tell you how much it costs. This measure is 23 inches. This chain sells for $2 per inch. Your cost is $46. Let me give you an idea of how much a matching bracelet would cost. Your wrist measures only 6 inches. At $2 per inch, your cost is only $12. You'd never be able to buy a ready-made bracelet that is only 6 inches. I can also put a charm on the necklace for you. Why don't you browse through the selection of 200 charms? If you find one you like, I'll put it on the necklace while you're waiting. Oh, okay. I'll take the bracelet and the necklace and pick out a charm for the necklace while you're making up the chain. What do you think about that chain? Oh, it's super. You know that they can make this up exactly to the length I need for my new dress. Is that right? Yeah. What do you think about this chain? Well, I'm just amazed at the price. I think that it's so reasonable that you should buy two. You should buy two of them. Oh, wonderful. So I'm leaping to page 58. And we're going to talk about demonstration. The Sam Pitts show, what we originally wanted to do, by the way, was have a real U.S. Postal Service truck drive on and postal guys take the mail out and the Postal Service wouldn't do it. So, God forbid they should have free advertising. But I still think that was a very cool demonstration for a very hard thing to demonstrate. So your page 58, which I have lost the white version of it, but you don't really need it, there are basically five kinds of demonstration that we can do. We can do the demonstration of profound importance. Did you show Houdini? Okay. Okay. So, the thing about that Houdini demo you know, it's the straight jacket escape, which is a straight jacket escape is a straight jacket escape. It's actually easier to do it hanging from a crane from the distance where nobody can get a good look at you than it is to do it right here on stage standing in front of you. But think how much cooler it is to see it while you are hanging from a crane. So there's a way to do demonstrations that make them much more important that do not really affect the nature of the demo. If you are going to show a video clip of you getting off of an airplane, I didn't dredge this out to show it from the old Glen Turner film, but so you are going to show yourself getting off of your plane. Here is how to make that much more important. There is a crowd of 100 people there waiting for you to get off the plane. That's what they do for politicians. Politicians flying into a city in order to go raise some money and they round up 100 people and take them out so the news cameras see a big crowd waiting for the person to get off the plane. You might have a crowd you can pull together, you can always rent a crowd, but if you are going to show that anyway you might as well show it in a way that's important. Product performance, so the Sam Pitts example is a way to do product performance even when we don't have a product like that that actually performs. Personal wizardry. So in our world that's often done with hot seats. So Jay Abraham, Gary Halver, me, we will demonstrate the wizardry by doing the hot seat and do four or five people and do their businesses. It's important to pick them. You do not just let anybody stand up and come up and do the hot seat. If you are doing it for this purpose it's important to screen them and pick them in advance when the faith healer smacks them on the head and they can walk and dance again, they were pre-chosen. It's not like anybody who wants to, they screen them back there and they let them come out because they have to be susceptible. They have to be, in our case if we are going to do a hot seat, they have to be like sane and rational and have something that actually can be helped. You don't want, when you have no control over it then it backfires. I did it at a, I mentioned it yesterday, I did it at a Dottie Walder seminar for speakers and authors and so she wanted me to do hot seats and I didn't get a chance to screen them. So the first one is a very nice lady who has written six books of poetry that are now stacked up in her garage and she wants to be the next chicken soup for the soul with her books of poetry that are already stacked up in her garage and thinks associations are going to sponsor her and hire her and I was much younger, I wasn't as nice as I am now. So I did a legitimate hot seat, you know, and she ran from the room weeping and was curled up outside with people consoling her and it cast a pall over the rest of the, you know, my version of it in the Peter Low speech was of course the Giorgio letters, demonstration. So we read parts of them, we showed you how the three step letter sequence works. A lot of people haven't seen it because I only did it when I did three hour long magnetic marketing presentations which were mostly association speaking gigs but I would have on stage, I would have a table with a couple of chairs and I would fairly early bring a couple people up out of the audience and I would have a fill in the blank template right out of the magnetic marketing system of the customer reactivation letter and I would sit them down at the table and have them fill in the blanks about their business right in front of everybody with no preparation and then I would show their letters thereby demonstrating that you can take this home and you don't have to know anything. All you have to do is understand your business and you can fill in these blanks just like mad libs and bingo you have a letter ready to go. I couldn't do it in the time frame I had at Peter's but I could do it if I had 300 insurance agents at Florida State Association insurance meeting. There's demonstration obviously of safety in numbers, pictures, video, huge crowds, there is a belief for presenters, speakers, that if they speak to big audiences they are more important, smarter and more valuable than if they speak to small audiences. Really stupid but still people see video or photo of you so you'll notice in all of our GKIC stuff we have this one wide angle shot of the audience from the back and it's shot like in an arc like the new curved TVs so it makes it look even bigger than it really is. I'm not any smarter because I'm standing in front of that and if I was standing in front of 12 people but people think there are and demonstration of happy outcomes which mostly has to be done through social proof and lifestyle visuals and so forth. Those are the five kinds of demonstration. The more of it you do if you are doing one to many presentations that you are delivering live or by video the more demonstration you do the better that you will do. So page where are we? 77 structure of promises. I'm still in the middle of the presentation by the way just in case anybody got a little lost. And so how do we structure promises? So the first issue is how big a promise are you going to make? I mentioned yesterday I have two corporate clients they both had this conversation very recently with me of gee can't we scale down the promises can't we back off the promises as long as you are willing to sell a lot less you can. Otherwise people don't just want to lose weight they want to lose weight while they sleep after having eaten everything they ever wanted to eat. That's how people want to lose weight. So the bigger the promise the bigger the result. And so there is a huge difference I have examples for you here these are all actually real examples. So these were all split tested. So there is an enormous difference between and it should be obvious learn the secrets of people who never get sick versus learn how to boost your immune system. Now if you let the academics write it or you let the lawyers write it they will write the second one. If you have any doubt in a split test it's the first one that wins. Grow twice the garden for half the cost and make your neighbors green with envy. Fifty tips for a more bountiful garden. Obviously we know which one wins. The first one even has the prime benefit in it which is sticking it up the butt of the neighbor. So big promise versus small promise you want to structure the biggest promise that you feel you can fulfill and won't put you in jail. The bigger the bolder the better. That promises versus implied promises. So there is a way to do promises that are direct if you are willing to do them make four thousand dollars a day sitting at your kitchen table on your underwear. That's a direct promise. And it has issues. The implied promise version of it is there are people making amazing incomes with a new kitchen table business. It's not as strong but it does not say you will or you can or even you can potentially. It just states a fact and requires you to translate in your head gee if there are people then I could. You have to decide whether you want to make a direct promise or whether you feel you need to make an implied promise. Another way to do implied promises that you will see done a lot is implied promises framed as questions. So in 1977 being a millionaire in America was still a big deal. So this was a headline and some copy from an ad I wrote that was extremely effective and the question the headline is you a millionaire. And there's statements of fact and then there's questions. Nowhere did I make a direct promise. I didn't say you could be a millionaire would be a millionaire might be a millionaire potentially could be a millionaire. I raised questions and made factual statements. Is it as strong as no but can't so this is this is one of a W.A.I.'s. Can you write a simple sales letter answer yes and you could be in debate. You may earn a great money writing just so that's a cross. Have you ever noticed there seems to be some people who can eat whatever they want as much as they want and never gain weight. It's an implied promise but we didn't say you could. We just said have you ever noticed. There are primary promises and secondary promises. So the primary promise is sales professionals you can put an end to cold prospecting once and for all. Now the secondary promises tend to get at what does that mean to different people who may be paying attention to the primary promise. So if we were back in the open the only thing you would have time to do is the primary promise. In the middle if we properly bought time for ourselves we have time to develop the idea and so we can deliver the secondary promises. One of which has to do with time one of which has to do with money one of which has to do with envy one of which has to do with state of mind and one of which has to do with family. So there's one two three four five yeah there's five secondary promises for the primary promise. I will tend usually to do three to five. So here's a couple of ways to think this through as you go to structure it. We spare no expense. So one is primary promise actual secondary benefits how I feel because of it secondary benefits and how others feel about me because of it secondary benefits. So this is a how others feel about me secondary benefit. Your spouse kids and friends will even notice the difference in you. The people that sell you can be home for dinner kind of thing. Ben does it Craig does it you know it's your dog will actually recognize you and not bite you. That's a that's in the third column here. That's how others feel about me because I have bought this on this do this. So there's three kinds of secondary benefits that you can build actual ones how the buyer feels and how others feel about the buyer. This is not your book. Another way to think about and think it through is the old wheel. You know this is like used to sell coaching so there's primary promise and then here's the different ways you can do secondary. Promises there could be a money one there could be a love sex one there could be a family one there could be a health one there can be a security one there can be a status one and there can be a lifestyle one. You only need one of each and you're over five. So primary secondary another promise structure is what's called stacking promises. This is important when you're time tight. So if you're time tight in a presentation like an infomercial or you really for whatever reason can only do a seven and a half minute video and that's what you could bitch yourself you can do then trying to stack two or three promises into one concise statement is what you want to do. Book titles often have to do this. Hence the Poeynhill's book title grow rich with peace of mind stacked title right win friends and influence people stacked title. Infusions conquer the chaos how to one grow a successful small business to without going crazy stacked title. The same thing when you write headlines when you write promise statements when you write claims you can concisely stack one two or three all within one sentence. When you are time tight the converse truth of this is if you waste a sentence delivering only one promise you probably could have delivered two promises in the same sentence if you sat and thought about it and worked at it. Also remember that what is it what something isn't constitutes promise is a structure of promise and is often is powerful or influential is saying what something is. You can't resource for you by the way another one of Masterson's books if you don't own the architecture of persuasion cheap book you can get it at Amazon it will help you with promise structure. Promise architecture of persuasion excuse me by Michael Masterson. So now let's talk about evergreen pitches. So yesterday we talked a little bit about evergreen assets by the way. So if you're going to go to the trouble to build a pitch and you're going to go to the trouble to build a group sales presentation that you are going to deliver however it is you're going to deliver it. You might as well base it on some of the appeals that never lose their punch that are literally evergreen. They work at the turn of the century they'll work at the turn of the next century they'll be working long after we're gone. We fall into a fairly limited number of categories. One is money financial opportunity and the deliverable maybe changes. So you guys probably remember the little screaming guy in the infomercial with you can make money with little tiny classified ads. Actually that same pitch today is for you can make money with Craigslist. There was a decade of very popular how to make money taking stuff to swap meets and selling it. That now is how to make money on eBay. It's the same pitch though. And so it's important also when you build your pitch today not just to narrow your range of knowledge to what's happened in the last five minutes because if you will dredge historically in the category you're in you will find the evergreen threads that have been in these pitches again and again and again and again. So as classified ads have gone away Craigslist is here same pitch when Craigslist goes away whatever the hell replaces it it'll be the same pitch. So financial is pretty much evergreen never goes away. People always want more money. They are not necessarily willing to work for more money but they always want more money. You will hardly ever get a hand raised if you take a survey or a poll. You have all the money you want and you don't want anymore. You may notice most of the billionaires in the country are 70 and 80 years old and they're working. They don't need money but they're still keeping score by money and if you put them all in a room and said you need more money most of them would say yes and the ones that didn't say yes to need if you said you want more money they would say we want more money. Weight loss never goes away. We're fat our fat will be fat forever more and now it's global because we're exporting our food. So well I'm serious a decade ago trying to find me a fat Chinese person in China now the place is full of them. They're eating KFC they're eating Taco Bell they're eating Pizza Hut the fat you can see them because they all come to Disney. So I'm serious I'm serious so it used to be you went to Disney and everybody from a foreign country was skinny and everybody from our country was the wide load people. Now everybody's a wide load person. You see them from the back and you think well there's an American and you like finally get past them and you turn around and you look at son of a gun they look like they're from India. How can they be fat? Well because we're exporting all our shit that's why. So weight loss now used to be really big here and not very big anywhere else. It's big everywhere and I do mean big everywhere. So weight loss right pitches haven't changed by the way the deliverable changes embarrassing and adequacy solved evergreen pitch. Everybody's very insecure they're easily agitated to be more insecure about almost anything and everything. So embarrassing and adequacy solved has been around since the beginning of time and it will continue to be around. How many have read the the great biography that came out late last year on Dale Carnegie on the whole Dale Carnegie business. Do you remember the title because I'm. American self help. Thank you self help Messiah. So a bunch of hands didn't go up. It's worth reading no matter what business you're in by the way but if you happen to be in the information business it's like mandatory reading. So it's a very it's academic. It's not exactly the most exciting thing you're ever going to read but it's a very well researched very good book on what is the longest enduring most successful self help thing in America Dale Carnegie and Dale Carnegie was the whole empire was all about shyness and timidity and a sense of social inadequacy and in business communication inadequacy. So it's an inadequacy play proactive. My client's eight hundred million dollar a year business is an inadequacy play. It's not as much about what the kid sees or the adult suffer sees you looking in the mirror for their own purposes. It's about feeling horrible about having acne because of what other people think. Listerines ad copywriters in nineteen twenty two invented bad breath. There was no such thing. It is not a medical condition. It was created by an advertising copywriter trying to sell a liquid that tastes like shit. So they invented bad breath. By the way there's a ton of those historical examples. Body odor was invented by an advertising copywriter. I don't remember them all and I didn't go research them all but pretty much if you name any embarrassing condition it was invented by the advertising copywriter charged with selling the solution. It had not been previously even thought about. Body odor up until nineteen forty eight in this country the average person took a shower or bath once a week. That was our social norm. Friday night baths. That was the social norm in America started to change right around nineteen fifty. So people really weren't concerned about body odor until an advertising guy made them concerned about body odor. Have you all read the making them believe book at least if you haven't got the course. Yes yes yes yes yes yes. Anybody not. Everybody should if you have to be in health care you definitely should. So I mean Brinkley really was selling the first cure for erectile dysfunction and that is all about sense of inadequacy. So this is this is an evergreen one. So if you think about Brinkley so that was nineteen what nineteen oh six nineteen ten somewhere in that neighborhood right. So that business opportunity hasn't gone anywhere. Right. It is bigger than ever. Come on. That see that kind of thing don't happen by accident. I have to think about that. So here this is a current magazine ad. It's had at least a three year run without a change. It's a very successful product. This is a huge industry and it's never going to thank you. If I keep beating the horse you'll eventually you know you'll get it. Superiority evergreen pitch. Martha Stewart exists because of the superiority appeal. I can be superior to my other women friends if I have a Martha Stewart luncheon and I cut down the trees and I make the napkins and they're in shapes of the lollows and etc. etc. That's what that is. Cosmopolitan magazine. I don't think you have these. I don't believe there's no page number on it so I don't believe you have it. So Cosmopolitan magazine created its cover and headline format in nineteen sixty two in two thousand and fourteen the headline the cover the one I just so glommed in Barnes and Noble took a look at one of the headlines is you'll how to give him the best sex he's ever had. And finally bit so maximum mind blowing orgasm she'll never forget and no man will ever top. You can go back to nineteen sixty two find the same stuff on the cover of Cosmopolitan hasn't changed. For men instructions real for women pretty much all you got to do show up naked. It's it. This is not rocket science in order to. This is actually a superiority play. People who are regular Starbucks customers feel superior to schmucks who buy their coffee at Dunkin Donuts and at the convenience store. It's a superiority play. It's because see this is about because I buy this I am this. That's what this is about status by purchase right will never go away disaster prep. I just had lunch with Harry Denton as Harry said if you want anybody to come scare them I'm your guy I can scare the shit out of him. So disaster prep has never gone away. Little known little known little known amway fact in nineteen fifty nine and nineteen sixty and we had three products they had a liquid cleaner they had laundry soap and they had backyard bomb shelters. Those are the three things they were selling right and they actually sold a fairly good number of them. And you can buy backyard bomb shelters today they're much more expensive and they come and install them and they stock them with food and air and light and water and I'm not I've never understood the concept because when you finally emerge now what it's all gone you have now consumed all the water all the food and you now emerge to a crater. The only other people are left to people who had bomb shelters they too have used everything up so it seems to me the biggest guy is going to eat the smallest weakest guy that's sort of the plan but gold so gold kind of goes away a little bit in cycles but it always comes out every time there's a bad economy why so you know guns so I always say you know if you visit somebody the way to tell if they're a Glenn Becklister is when they raise their garage door half of the garage is full of gold and half of the garage is full of guns and there's a guy sitting in a lawn chair with a gun in his lap guarding it and if they're not full of guns it's full of survival food so now you got a Glenn Becklister. It's a huge market hasn't changed won't go away it ebbs and flows a little bit with conditions but it never really goes away an end of America huge hit against an enemy evergreen category. As long as somebody has an enemy there will be stuff they buy because of that enemy so if you go back and look at the $4000 day in your underwear ad in the archives collection you'll find we got a big bump as soon as we put a paragraph at the bottom of it about Hillary coming to get your money. Now it would be about Obama coming to get your money and in however many years it is I have a countdown thing in my office but I don't have it my head when whoever it is it takes a place it will be about that person coming to get your money and it will still get the bump. So enemy and then clever combinations thereof this by the way is the current end of America piece and so this is the same pitch I just want to show you how little it takes to update anything so this is the same end of America pitch that Stansbury has now used for three years the direct mail piece. I want to show you the current changes that make it current. One he's picked a new date for the end of America because lo and behold it didn't end on the last three dates so now it's July 1st 2014 which by the way you make a point laugh really enjoy this seminar because July 1st baby it's all over right and there's a new sticker on it which really isn't a sticker it's just printed to look like a sticker it's got a picture of Obama our enemy and it says new Obama law first look new currency law goes into effect July 1st right and it appears in two places the rest of this is unchanged same pitch unchanged since the beginning and they're still mailing a crap out of it and it's still working. So those are your categories of evergreen appeals and you ought to consider them when you go build any presentation you might as well hook it on build it on a foundation on one of the appeals that never dies that is reliable when you structure a presentation. Now I'm back to your manual on page 81 so those of you that were grumpy about it. So I call this the lost secret of 7 to 21 times so we used to get this everybody in my business understood it about the training side of our business so we always sold with it too the 7 to 21 times repetition so if you move into a new house like an idiot so this is part of the pitch to self-cassette programs because you're going to go listen to them 7 to 21 times which then you did a value proposition by the way so there's six cassettes in an album but you have to listen to each one 7 to 21 times so even if you only do 7 that's 42 so if you divide $60 by 42 it's a lot less than it is if you divide $60 by 6 so it's part of the value proposition and the reason that's important is because if you move into a new house then depending upon how quick a study you are but at least for seven nights like an idiot you will be in the dark reaching for the light switches where they were in the old house most people will be reaching for the light switches where they were in the old house for 21 nights in a row before their hand and mind readjusts and automatically goes to the light switch where it is in a new house therefore you need 7 to 21 repetitions of any input that you want to actually stick and be held and be able to be used okay so that was the pitch so we all understood it well most people don't know is not only is it a good pitch it's legitimate it's actually true so well it's not necessary from the standpoint of a pitch but it is actually true it is how we get things we get them through repetition and we get them even when we don't try so everybody in the room knows lyrics to some song you made no attempt whatsoever to memorize it's not like you sat there and tried to memorize it but you know them and at least with a prompt you can probably do them it's why it's why name that tune works you can hear the first few bars of a song and you know what the song is you didn't try and do that it happened to you automatically through repetition so what happens automatically by accident without you trying to do it if you just apply it to what you're trying to do this is how I got through Latin in high school which by the way so the first dumb mistake is taking Latin some vague thought that I wanted to be a veterinarian and at the time you know you still needed a Latin to get through vet school or medical school it was not that I was planning on being a priest I was planning on being a veterinarian and we had Spanish French and Latin and the hottest teacher was Latin so there was two there was two means of making that decision it turns out to be completely useless every once in a while I can get covatus into a conversation but but so here's how I get through Latin so Latin our system anyway you had a weekly quiz and you had a monthly test the weekly quiz was vocabulary English word Latin word Latin English word can be memorized the monthly test you had to use the words in present tense past tense future tense in sentences the grade quizzes counted for half the grade tests counted for half the grade that means if you ace the quizzes you could flunk the test and get a C that's me the memorization was recording the vocabulary and then playing it on an audio cassette over and over and over again until the quiz including while I slept with a little pillow speaker ace every single quiz flunked every single test just sat there because I had an idea right three or four times accused of cheating on the quizzes because how could you ace the quiz and flunk the test kept explaining to them because to do the test you have to be able to use this crap to ace the quiz you only have to know it I know it I memorized it I just can't use it so our minds really do work this way stuff sticks through repetition so when you build a presentation you want seven to twenty one repetitions of the most important things in them internally during the course of the presentation so if you go back through the script samples I've given you and you hit upon a big idea an important point made early and now you stick count how many times it's restated almost identically during the course of that presentation you're going to hit seven to twenty one I don't miss because I make sure I don't miss so how do you say it seven to twenty one times in a presentation without being horribly club clumsy about it although better to be horribly clumsy about it than not do it one you build it into your very open you build it into your open tease your open promise your open assertions as early in the presentation as you possibly can you put the most important thing you want to drive home that has to be accepted in order for them to buy you can say it by direct assertion you can bury it inside a demonstration and have an opportunity to say it again show it prove it you can bury it inside testimonials so the testimonials either say it or they get close to it or they reinforce it so you can deliver it inside testimonials you can put it inside a story you can put it inside the headline of a story so the way you announce a story or start a story allows you to say it again so if our big idea is big money and big chunks we can put it every place I've named so far we can put it in an opening we can say it as a direct assertion with this system you'll get big money and big chunks we can put it in some kind of demonstration we could have made all those checks he took out of the envelopes five thousand dollar checks instead of nineteen dollar checks and we have demonstrated big money and big chunks we can have testimonials say I never thought thought only rich people and doctors and lawyers got big money and big chunks but now I so we have a testimonial say the headline of the story here's a story of a desperate Iowa housewife who discovered how to get big money and big chunks all right and then we tell her story has the punchline of a story at the end as you can see Judy discovered how to get big money in big chunks we can tie it to a proffer of facts so if this is if our big idea is get your teeth three shades wider and just three days we can tie it to the fact right so the Harvard study showed that nine out of ten dentists recommend x which is exactly why you can rely on x which is they didn't say you could which is exactly why so if we say it's exactly why are my son okay it's exactly why you can rely on x to get your teeth three shades wider in just three days we can obviously put it inside the guarantee if you don't get big money and big chunks within 89 days you can return everything so we can put it in the guarantee and we can stick it in a trial clause so there's ten ways we can try and get our repetition within the presentation and they it's not the same as just repeating it over and over and over again now one to many at a distance and even in a room is handicapped by the fact that often you are speed selling and they do not have a firm grip on what it is they are buying there is an impulse to the purchase right a guy he bought over the years at the success events he bought magnetic marketing nine times he didn't realize it until like the 10th year and he's telling me I bought it in 1996 I bought it 1998 about it from he was moving and finally it all came together all still shrink wrapped of course okay but he just got caught up in the pitch and ran to the back of the room and bought it never once thinking I was here three years ago same guy same suit same slides I wonder if it's the same product right so so they were we're speed selling they're buying on impulse they often don't know exactly what they're buying that's somewhat of a handicap so we got to implant the big idea that drives the purchase through repetition there's also a thing about it being a closed loop so so how many have read the book sham oh not near enough okay I forget the author doesn't make any difference and I will preface it by saying it's only about 70 right and in places it is profoundly unfair but it is still really everybody ought to read it just kind of substitute what goes on in your industry to this if you are in any way shape or form my industry that's what it's written about so it is a attack on it is an attack on the self-help industry that's what it is and it's a little dated now some but Tony's in it and Barbara d'Angelo's is in it and John Gray you know the Venus Mars guy so there's a lot of people you know who are who are critically featured in the book and so sham stands for self-help something movement is what it's an abbreviation for so the reason to read it beyond fun if you have sort of a perverse idea of fun is that he does accurately reveal a lot of the structural stuff about how self-help motivation and success is sold to people and his background is he was at Rodale for many years as a copywriter so he was at Rodale it'd be like being at boardroom you know whatever so you get the book it's in its name is Steve Salerno S.A. L.E.R.N.O. you can get the book it's paperback you can get it at Amazon so one of the things that he reveals is there is a circular closed loop to to this to this structure so I'll just show you so this is his criticism this is not to say it's false there self-help priests that can't be disputed because they are circular the guru is saying the same thing in different ways at the start and end of the sentence the conclusion restates the promise and so if you start to get analytical about this you will see it done again and again and again the conclusion proves the promise and the conclusion is only a restatement of the promise so here's a keywords for example or that being true so here's a sham example incredibly most sales professionals choose to be miserable while a few choose to prosper and enjoy selling now jump to the end I won't read the middle first that fact guarantees that you can choose freedom from prospecting or drudgery while others choose misery right it's a big circle we're just going around a circle so now read the whole thing incredibly most sales professionals choose to be miserable while a few choose to prosper and enjoy selling there's nothing more discouraging to sales professionals than endless difficult cold prospecting most simply resign themselves to it but those who discover magnetic marketing break free of it never waste a minute be an annoying pester set of a welcome guest again that's how you two can transform your experience in selling that fact what fact be analytical for a second what fact that fact guarantees that you could choose freedom from prospecting drudgery while others choose misery which is a restatement of the first sense of the paragraph right it's a oh yeah it's beautiful thank you very much it's a it's a self-proving circular piece of language that didn't go anywhere and didn't really establish anything so when you build your seven to 21 repetitions you can build them as closed loops which actually gets you another repetition because you are proving the promise with a restatement of the promise and you are calling the middle that said nothing the fact that proves the promise when you go to rep a repeat and reinforce you can do it verbally right so somehow it's done verbally you can do it visually live or video right and you can do it in print if you have print by the way i showed you in a split test yesterday a online order form where the only change they made was adding two little tiny little testimonials to a little blank space left at the bottom of the order form and again i i've gotten a little lazy about it over the years but it's amazing how many order forms don't have any testimonials on them particularly speakers who use them live my peter low form had testimonials on the back if they're going to stand in line waiting and they're thinking and maybe they're going to walk away they might as well read a testimonial and the testimonials reinforce the key promises so there's few rules sort of you do have this i'm all the way up to 84 one is you want to bookend one way or another because the law primacy of recites he says most people if you bring them up on stage and you give them a list of six random numbers one 48 12 nine most people can remember the first couple and the last couple they can't get the ones in the middle and if they get the ones in the middle they get them in the wrong order it's called primacy and recency so and the longer the presentation the more problematic this is right because they can only remember what you started with and they what you're ending with and they can't remember anything in the middle but it's even true of a short paragraph so you want to bookend and bookend and bookend and bookend when you do your repetition sometimes you are going to have to stop and summarize that points within a long presentation you have to bring people so if you made five promises so you will see in my i will get to it i think you will see in the magnetic marketing speech so i had three acts right i had three things that we talked about message market media there's a summary statement okay okay now we have got message taken care of now we okay now we have message and market now we will go to be so you have to stop and put what you've done together for them so okay now i got all four of those things has a primacy now he's going to talk again and he's going to stop with a point and so i'll remember this and i can remember where he winds up i'm just kind of riding along in between open close start and stop one secondary promise at a time always connecting them back to the primary promise because if you do four or five secondaries they forgot the primary if you don't keep making that hook so the mistakes most often made in the middle are number one the whole thing's just too damn complicated right the people that make this mistake the most are people who for one reason or another feel compelled to teach they have convinced themselves for one reason or another like gee these people came to a seminar so we should give them a seminar you know they came to a seminar but you're doing sales presentation all right those are two entirely different things all right they came to an interview as a webcast yes but you're doing a sales presentation so teachers people who like to teach feel some ethical guilt ridden need to teach for whatever reason the teachers get themselves in real trouble all right because they make the whole thing way too complicated that anybody is ready for at their first exposure the guy you saw in the video in my video sales letter phil grow so phil first of all he's an engineer right he's an engineer background so he thinks like an engineer and he sounds like an engineer but that's reassuring to a lot of people i like that part of him but turned loose in a preview seminar he teaches and he teaches all the complicated stuff 37 ways to make a deal he tries to teach all 37 of them i'm going phil they can't take it that's a year's worth of coaching they just want to know that you got 37 ways and they can get a deal they don't we don't need to teach it it's in the way so you can make it way too complicated so people always ask me would i do it differently today why did in the home magnetic marketing speech once we get to media the only example i shows direct mail and mostly i talk about direct mail reason for that all right they can't handle more on one media if i start talking to him about direct mail and email and broadcast facts and everything i've just made a way too complicated to make the sale let's say why three steps there's no magic in the three steps it can be five it can be 47 has you guys all know all right a three is a nice number people can remember three i have a whole framework of three in that speech message market match three step letter i have a whole series of threes right people can remember threes it also works with the dunning letter story the collection industry story which is three steps but not everybody uses three steps but if i so here's how you do a three step and then if you want to do a five step and you could do a seventeen step and you could integrate email and you could integrate phone calls i'm not gonna sell shed all right i sold because they got it well okay three steps a i could probably do three steps it's it's a little taxing but i could probably do three i haven't run out of fingers when i count them i got fingers left over all right i kind of get it all right so you can over complicate the middle too many ideas too much information too much teaching and to play fair about this for the most part there's very little that you can do in one presentation of any length that the overwhelming majority of people are actually going to be able to take go and implement and get any results for right you're smoking crack all right they can't do it all right so i know tony claims he can you know touch them on the head and give them an organism and change their lives forever and god bless them but uh two days later they're right back to where they were the best we can do is we can create awareness of new method new opportunity new solution new way of thinking about something we can create interest in it and then we can provide some sort of opportunity to implement over time whether that's buying the toolkit or be it so so if i'm a playing fair standpoint you do no one any favors by just burying their butt in information they won't go use it anyway they can't my theft control speaker when i got him as a client he i took his his his one day seminar and i made three days out of it he said jack we got it you know they can't be lost by the time you're at one o'clock in the afternoon so they get lost that's the second big mistake so when things aren't hooked together for them if you leave it up to them to make the connections they will get lost so they you need structure because they need structure okay too much information not enough emotion they only need to know enough to buy the rest of the time ought to be motivating them about buying either by scaring the crap out of them if you wish or painting them the wonderful picture of how things will be but so whether you like it or not if you are a group presenter you are a motivational speaker you may not like the term anymore than i like the term but that's what you are so they only need to know enough to buy they don't need to know any more than that you gain no leverage by giving them any more knowledge than what they need to buy all you do is waste time cost yourself money and deprive them of whatever opportunity it is that you are presenting to them so you got to get to emotion no fun buying is supposed to be fun people shop for fun you might not i might not say walmart's entire business is because it is an entertainment for low income people and that's by the way why their sales right now have taken a big dip because low income people have less discretionary money price of gas is up price of food is up so a higher percentage of their income is going to basic necessities so now they go to the walmart store three times a week and they actually pretty much only get what they went there to get that they need and they go home so they get toilet paper and they get some frozen pizzas and you know they get a bottle of juice and they go home walmart's whole business is about them coming three times a week to get those things but then being there for an hour and a half has their entertainment they wander through the store and they find a dvd and they might want to watch and they find a new kitchen gadget and they find that's their entertainment and so shopping for them is fun for you going to neiman marcus might be fun or going to nordstrom's might be fun all right for me going to a horse auction is fun i'm shopping i'm buying oh good i feel good because i bought and i had fun the reason disney sells so much stuff is because people have fun while they are buying they have completely integrated shopping with entertainment the whole thing you can't get out of an attraction without going through the store you can't the whole thing is one giant the world show how many of you been to the world showcase part of epcot let's do it the other way how many haven't been really honest to god where are you people doing how can that be so you got to go so world showcase is a bunch of countries okay so i explain this every once in a while to carla it would be ever so nice to go over i said no see we can go to orlando and in half a day we can do 22 countries how many more do you need to visit we just we'd be at the pool in the afternoon if you want to you know you go to spa and go get a massage we knocked out europe asia you know africa we bloom all out you know so world showcase is countries that's what it is little miniature landmark little main street village area of paris and london and istanbul and you know whatever else it stores their gift shops that's what they are there's one movie in each one that's the only thing that's there that isn't about buying stuff then there's restaurants and their stores and it's all imported stuff from the country that it represents so you know everything we bought at herods in london and slept home and drug through customs and all that stuff i could buy right there in england i've proven it right there in england and disney they'll ship it to my house and they'll throw in a free mickey mouse watch so the whole thing is shopping they have made a day of shopping and they have people confused they don't realize they're shopping even they think they're being entertained and they're having a ball and somehow they wind up with 18 bags full of stuff all right because that's what it is it's ingenious it's a mall that nobody really thinks of as a mall that's what it is it's clever so people buy more happily when they have some fun doing it so your webcast your webinar your meeting ought not be too serious from beginning to end it's okay to have a little fun with people if they're smart customers it's even okay to let them have a little inside baseball to have to let them understand a little bit about what's happening to them so there ought to be fun you want to another mistake is that they actually tell things and tell too much about things that they raise doubts rather than create reassurance so when phil teaches the 37 ways that you can get a deal he isn't reassuring the people we put in the room he's raising a bunch of doubts in the people we put in the room because now it sounds really complicated and how am I going to pick which ones do I have to do all of them and and and and all of the technical language comes out of his mouth appraisals and property evaluations and lenders and the federal law 1803 slash x y z 42 that was passed that covers land trusts had everybody's gone wait a minute I thought this was big bunny in big chunks this don't sound like big buddy in big chunks to me uh-oh you know this guy's a rocket scientist I'm going no no no we need to reassure people they need to feel ever more secure that they can do this that it's painless that you could figure it out that you're going to have all this help so a big mistake is actually raising doubt rather than further erasing doubt so next category of stuff has to do with bridges and segways I'm on 86 so a lot of people ask about this I just picked off my old friend Steve Miller's question but a lot of people ask how do you transition from primary content to selling that's his question so the first part of that answer is you don't because you should have been selling the entire time the idea that there is a border so you you got to be put in here you got to understand no borders okay you guys only thought there was a border all right so the idea that there is a border of somehow between primary content and selling all that does is get you in trouble all right the worst version of it is the person who delivers content content content content content and then they sort of physiologically change and now they deliver a commercial here's what happens when the person has a choice so that's tv entertainment entertainment entertainment commercial what does everybody do when the commercial comes on they go get food they go get a beer they go to the bathroom and get rid of the food so on the next break they can get some more food all right so they're either in the kitchen or they're in the can every time the commercials come on unless they have a way to zoop past them because they recorded the show instead of watching the show live all right the only time anybody voluntarily so some people are too lazy to get up off the couch so they sit there but the only time people like voluntarily want to see the commercials is when super ball yeah there's a ton of people who now skip the game all right so they yack they make sandwiches they go to the can during the game and then all the commercials are on i mean the last four years i've been someplace where people watch the super bowl you actually hear it in the house hey hey the commercials are on come in everybody runs in to watch the commercials i'm going i thought this was a football game you know and i hate halftime so so if it doesn't work for tv why would you do it to yourself so what happens if you do content content content whoops commercial is they mentally disconnect right then and so they go to the mental toilet and they think about something completely unrelated to what you've been talking about and incidentally start forgetting all of the points you made at a rapid pace so they now go think about sex or they think about the leaky faucet or they think about whatever right they mentally leave okay and when you stop giving the commercial and you start telling a story or something they sort of blue these back to content so that's no good furthermore the thought that there are boundaries is a false thought every one to many selling presentation is a selling presentation does it have content in it yes are people capable of confusing it to our advantage yes just like people who spend half a day in the country's at disney don't think they're in a shopping mall okay people can see your presentation and think think they're in a content presentation so we got two things from my presentations on the success events during the nine years that i did them white male about them most of which went to peter first so we got complaints more than any other speaker by the way so i consistently held the record in the most number of people who would take the time to write a letter to complain about something all right so i i got those a whole variety but but most of them predictable you could guess right i also got the most number of letters complaining essentially about the entire event by praising the fact that finally this guy we never heard of at the end of the day gave us more content right than anybody else all added together i have a big stack of them right i used to send the ones that specifically reference zig i used to send them to zig with a post-it note on it said see you know um once we were buddies um so people thought they got more content from my presentation than they got from any other presentation not so right especially not usable content i mean you got to be really astute to leave from that presentation and go home and do anything you you've got to be sharp as a tack all right but because i did all the stuff we talked about earlier with ideas that stick i did numbers i did all this stuff see they thought they were getting content my goal was to give them not one minute of usable content i'm there to sell stuff i'm very clear about why i'm there all right i made that mistake my first year speaking and i got it completely out of my system so i thought if you just give them a ton of great content they can clearly see you have taught them 512 secrets of the meaning of life on this planet you can then just say over there some of my stuff and they'll all run over there and get it okay so i've made that mistake most a lot of people never outgrow that mistake by the way they keep trying to make the content better thinking that that will cause people to be smart enough to now buy their stuff people ain't that smart right they're just not so all you do with that is defeat you and defeat them so i made that mistake right i made the mistake of trying to do the mix and have the borders and all that and about a year i got it all out of my system and i said i don't care 90 minutes four hours eight hours whatever you want me to do when i sit down and build it if i build a new one or i tweak what i've got i'm building a sales presentation that's what i'm building the main way to do it so people can't tell the difference is by telling them what to do and what not to do but never telling them how to do it and then just continuing to reinforce show and demonstrate the what to do and the what not to do but you never really get to the how to do it so if you go to magnetic marketing speech you see the whole conversation within message about a unique selling proposition and how you need a unique selling proposition and the fact that nobody has one and here's a great example which is dominoes and when i'm all done here's what people know they know they need one they know they ain't got one they have no earthly idea how to go sit down with a pad and a pen and build one none zero nada i didn't give them any templates i didn't give them a 22 examples i didn't tell them what should be first and what should be second i didn't do any of that but they think they got all kinds of stuff so that's what you want to do so there's no boundaries there are however needed bridges and segues from one place to another from what is perceived to be content to what will now be perceived has called action we do need to help them get from place to place but you ought to be very clear that you're actually moving them from a selling place to another selling place you are not moving them from a content place to a selling place there is no content place there's only a content place they think is a content place and that disney actually does put a movie in every one of these places and it's generosity on their part all right because they could just as easily have another store in there and everybody would be just as happy by the so we're bridging and segueing from selling to selling to selling some people will feel this is unfair i again remind you the least fair thing in the world is to get somebody all lathered up about fixing a problem or capitalizing on an opportunity and send them home away from you lathered up with no means of following through what could be less fair than that so if you don't sell them your thing you wasted their time so you got to get past any attitudinal things you have about this so your page 86 here's how we do bridges and segways the difference basically is that a segue is an uninterrupted transition it feels completely seamless nobody would ever really recognize that you were moving them a bridge is a structural device and mostly you are acknowledging here's a bridge come with me we are now going from here to here that's really the difference the thing you're accomplishing is the same all right so how to get from the end of an idea to the next idea some best options one a bridge that is a summary statement plus the introduction of the next idea now that we have the eradicated bad breath if you want to have a lot of sex we must also eradicate body odor well now we have moved from one to the other and we're going to sell a box of stuff that includes mouthwash and deodorant and soap another bridge structure is trial clothes that their response becomes the reason for being now that we have shown you how to eliminate bad breath if you can have great breath all the time wouldn't you love also to have a wonderful smelling body so you can actually get laid yes i would all right segue is a mini story that ties idea a to idea b so we could not tell a story about the person who now has wonderful breath and still can't get away and he now discovered actually that the better his breath spells the more noticeable it is that is body stinks and so we have to fix both these problems segue is a mini demo i'll stop a mini demo that ties idea a to idea b i was good to do it by the way it's like no no no we don't really have time anyway the last is a testimonial that ties idea a to idea b so the testimony will completes the loop of the first idea and raises the second idea right how to get from one segment to the next you build a bridge of a summary plus a tease of what's coming up next so it's perfectly fine to say okay that wraps up x right now here's the next amazing you're going to discover so that wraps up how to get money in big big money in big chunks from easy transactions now we're going to talk about how to multiply the money you get through investing it's perfectly okay to say it to let them get okay we got we built this box we filled it up with cool stuff we all now understand it know it what you're really standing say is against you now know how to build a great marketing message they don't but they'll not and say to themselves they do because i just summarized it and told them they do and now we're going to talk about how to figure out who you should deliver the message to new box it's fine to do that it's done a lot okay so when you're actually going from content to commercial and it is going to be noticeable so now we finally get to the answer steve wanted so if you're actually going to go from content to commercial in a way that is noticeable now again keep in mind you're really going from perceived content to commercial you're never going from real content to commercial because you shouldn't be delivering any real content but if it's going to be seen felt recognized then here's the way to do it so number one which is used more often than anything else is a simple reason why and the benefit here is the reason why can include an enormous amount of truth so the old version of this was the seven to one twenty one times repetition arguments stated one way or another so it was some version of maybe with a trial close okay we've done this this and this and aren't you feeling great you're like you can't wait to get home and apply this to your isn't if you want to do trial close isn't that right everybody feels that way raise your hand right so now here's the bad news okay and now there's a thing about how bad your memory is which it's terrible all right so x percentage of you three days from now you will have forgotten everything I said except for the jokes and seven days from now you won't remember who I was and 14 days from now you want to remember haven't been here let alone what the hell we talked about and those of you that took a bunch of notes here's what happens with notes you go home you got a place everybody's got a place where they put all the notes from all the seminars they go to it's either under the bed it's out in the garage in boxes it's in them nice boxes you get it off as max that you have to assemble yourself with the lids you got a whole stack of them somewhere with all the notes it came to the seminars and it's where the notes from this seminar are going to go to right it's there for now we have to solve that problem for people right so the the way we used to solve it was here's all the stuff you need to listen to and you need to listen to it seven and 21 times and then it'll be permanently embedded in your psychology and you'll be able so we don't tell that anymore because if I tell you you have to listen to it seven and 21 times you don't buy it all right it's hard enough to get you to buy it to listen to it once right because you really just want the thing done for you all right so the new hook to that is more the toolkit so it's either tools or it's done for you so the solution now to this problem that you are good at leave here all lathered up and you think you got it but you don't and by the time you get where you are that you're going to try and use it you will not remember how to use it so if we just taught you how to fix your kids and how to have happy healthy respectful teenagers who eagerly clean their room and don't cuss at you anymore and and here's the scripts and we showed you by the time you actually get a chance to use it on the teenager you won't remember how to do it and you'll bungle it all so here's the solution to that when we self fix the phone my client j guyer we make a big point out of okay now you got it right you understand how horrible it is and you understand what a good phone call supposed to sound like and all that but if you go try and do this yourself here's what's going to happen the staff is going to either quit or they are going to kill you right because you will bungle it sometimes we'll tell the mike vance piss in the pool story which google vance and then search for piss in the pool if you don't know the story so one way or another we in we invalidate what they think they're good to be able to do and now we have a solution it used to be spaced repetition learning then magnetic marketing is all about a toolkit that's so brain dead that you can't help but use it just page out use it just the way it is filling a few blanks or if you're selling done for you it's done for you if you're selling coaching it's coaching but the setup is really the same so there's a reason why bridge right another bridge is the simplest one which used to work a lot is just more right get a good time we had a ball we just did this two-day seminar on wealth guess what there's a 36 module 12 month thing more of it you want more of it you want to take me home with you to be with you night and day don't you right so there's a more argument right there's the if bridge i use this a lot when i'm selling so there it's really a series of little trial closes i don't make usually make people raise their hands sometimes i do but there's the if you have seen great value if you have all gotten at least one million dollar idea that you're good then there is more so there's the if bridge there's the in your service bridge which is now it links to the reason why but it's the reason why that i'm about to sell you this stuff is because i am deeply concerned that you are going to be able to implement and i will lose sleep and perhaps over time become suicidal if i think that all i'm doing is getting you lathered up and leaving you unable to implement so it's sort of the mission statement thing there's the divide and conquer bridge which is some version of we reached the point where everybody is going to identify themselves and there are really x kinds of people and they reveal themselves by x kinds of responses to where we are at this moment so there's this kind of people and they are already at the back of the room trying to find the resources all right then there's these kinds so it's these kind of people so the first time i really saw it platform selling and i saw divide and conquer it blew me away and it was mr. gentile lovable zig-ziggler who did it and it is brutally manipulative and i was in awe and i was very young but i got it instantly he's speaking at a huge amway rally now you have to understand multi-level everybody recruits somebody else right that's the deal all right it's not like direct selling where maybe there's two levels of MLM everybody's recruit and at the big rallies everybody's got somebody with them unless they're a real loser who they who they are either trying to recruit or they have just recruited right so they're there with somebody who they are trying to lead so zig's clothes is you notice if you turn around and look while we've been doing this to facilitate the lines at the back of the room they have put up three different color flags and they have they put up these flags i watch them do it and they're the kind that used to have at gas stations you know or car washes they're the triangle flags on a string red white and blue it's great so that's because there's three lines back there to get three different packages of resources so you want to be in the white line if you're just kind of messing around with this you don't really know if you're going to get serious you're really kind of a spare time make a few extra bucks not that there's anything wrong with that he said it differently than me but but not much you want to be in the white line and you're going to get this you didn't mention price by the way now if you're a little more serious and you know that you want to build a business and you're going to be an organizational leader and you're going to help other people build but you want to get in the blue line and you're going to get this now if you're a real leader and you're really going to lead people to new lives of prospects you're going to get under the red flags and you're going to get this so exactly who is going to get in the white flag right the equivalent of what he's really saying is if you have a really really really tiny dick and you don't mind everybody knowing it get in the white line okay that's what he's really said I said that to him I told him this whole story have he said this I said so my translation of what you said he says I would never say that he said he probably would have sold a little more merchandise but so so nobody's going to get in the white line right so actually this day there were about eight ten people who got in the white line they all moved over okay they like moved over right and a bunch of the people in the blue line who started in the blue line they moved over because the longest line is the red line how can you be there with somebody you're supposed to lead and not get in the leader line you've got to get in the leader line it's sales by extortion it was magnificent I watched this I'm a kid I'm watching this I'm thinking this is the coolest pickpocket thing scheme he's left them no choice with this this is the greatest thing I've ever seen right so I've never done it with the lines I would love to do it with the lines but I've never done it but you do it verbally and you can do it as the bridge right so some ways to soften the bridge should you wish to do so one is a summary story between the middle and the bridge so if you look at the magnetic marketing speech the Alda plumber story is between the middle and the bridge to a visible commercial to a visible call to action and it and the story does several things it summarizes all of the things I told them they're going to be able to do in the context of one story it's funny so it softens the transition into the hard cta and it sets up the reason to have the resources you can do a summary testimonial between the middle and the bridge so instead of a story it can be a story told about a person which summarizes the main points you made and gets us into the bridge or it can be in that person's voice so you could show a video you could go to a video clip
